operating_state: Total revenue declined 2% to $49.0 billion as commercial aerospace recovery at Collins and Pratt & Whitney was offset by defense declines at RMD and RIS due to supply chain constraints and anticipated production decreases. Commercial aerospace represented 40% of total sales versus 34% prior year, driven by higher aftermarket sales and OEM volumes from increased flight hours and aircraft utilization.
cost_structure: Gross margin improved to 20.6% from 19.1% as favorable commercial aftermarket mix more than offset organic cost increases. Operating margin expanded to 8.0% from 7.7% despite increased expenses and defense margin compression, with RMD margins declining due to unfavorable program mix and supply chain pressures. Net unfavorable EAC adjustments across defense programs reflected continued operational headwinds.
material_events: Executed $2.4 billion in share repurchases and paid $2.3 billion in dividends with $0.55 quarterly dividend declared for December 2022. Recorded $210 million in Russia-related charges including asset impairments and credit losses, while disposing of two non-core Collins businesses. Major defense contract awards included hypersonic missile and AMRAAM contracts.
macro_exposures: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act research expenditure capitalization requirements resulted in $1.5 billion incremental tax payments with additional amounts expected in Q4. Russia sanctions created advance payments from Middle East customer where regulatory approvals are no longer expected. Turkey supply chain risks persist with sole-sourced aerospace components subject to potential U.S. sanctions, while China indicated potential sanctions against CEO and company related to Taiwan foreign military sales.
forward_outlook: Management reaffirmed full-year sales and adjusted earnings guidance while updating free cash flow outlook downward due to tax payment impacts. Continued commercial aerospace recovery expected driven by fundamental air travel demand, though timing of full capacity restoration remains uncertain. Defense operations positioned for sustained performance with backlog growth supported by geopolitical demand drivers.
uncertainty_profile: COVID-19 recovery timeline for commercial air traffic to exceed pre-pandemic levels remains uncertain. Supply chain constraints and labor market pressures continue impacting defense program execution and cost estimates. Geopolitical tensions create ongoing risks including potential China sanctions, Russia-Ukraine conflict impacts, and Turkish supplier dependencies facing potential U.S. sanctions disruption.
